title: Design Case Studies — Portfolio Storytelling
content: A case study tells the story of a design project, demonstrating process, thinking, and impact. Structure: 1) Overview — title, one-line summary, role, team, timeline, key outcome (the hook). 2) Challenge — business context, problem statement, user needs, constraints, why it mattered. 3) Process — research methods and findings, ideation and exploration (show breadth), key decisions and rationale (show depth), iteration from feedback/testing. 4) Solution — final design walkthrough, key features, how it addresses the challenge, design system considerations. 5) Impact — quantitative results, qualitative feedback, business impact, what you'd do differently. 6) Reflection — learnings, challenges overcome, skills developed. Visual storytelling: show the journey not just the final product, include sketches/wireframes/iterations, use before/after comparisons, annotate key decisions. Writing: first person for your contributions, be specific about your role vs team, quantify impact, keep it scannable and edit ruthlessly. Lead with the most impressive outcome.

title: Design Rationale — Connecting Decisions to Evidence
content: Design rationale documents the reasoning behind decisions, preventing future teams from reversing good choices without understanding why. Structure: 1) Decision — what was chosen, specifically. 2) Context — what problem or need prompted this. 3) Options considered — alternatives explored with brief descriptions. 4) Evidence — user research, data, best practices, competitive analysis, testing that informed the choice. 5) Reasoning — why this option over alternatives, connected to user needs, business goals, design principles, technical feasibility. 6) Trade-offs — known compromises, what was deprioritised and why. 7) Validation plan — how you'll know if the decision was right, what metrics or feedback will confirm. When to write: major direction decisions, departures from patterns, controversial choices, decisions that will be questioned later. Write during the decision, not after. Keep concise but complete. Store alongside design files. Quality test: connects to user needs (not designer preference), references evidence, acknowledges trade-offs, is useful months later.

title: Design System Adoption — Driving Consistent Usage
content: Adoption strategy has four pillars: Awareness (launch demos, documentation site with search, regular changelog, showcase exemplary projects), Education (getting started guides for designers and developers, component usage guidelines, workshop series, office hours), Enablement (Figma library with setup instructions, code packages, templates/starter kits, migration guides), Incentives (celebrate adopting teams, track metrics, make the system easier than custom work, include usage in review criteria). Measuring adoption: component usage percentage in production, custom/override style count, support question volume (should decrease), time-to-implement (should decrease), consistency audit scores. Common barriers: system doesn't cover needs, documentation incomplete, components too rigid, breaking changes too frequent, no contribution path. Overcoming resistance: listen to objections (they reveal real gaps), offer migration support not mandates, show productivity gains with data, start with willing teams, make contributing easy. Treat the design system as a product with users — invest in documentation as much as components.

title: Design Token Audit — Consistency and Coverage Analysis
content: A token audit identifies inconsistencies, gaps, and hard-coded values across a product. Scope: Token coverage (what percentage of visual properties use tokens? Which are hard-coded? Are correct tiers used — global vs semantic vs component?), Token consistency (same tokens for same purposes? Redundant tokens with different names? Deprecated tokens still used?), Token gaps (values that should be tokens but aren't? Use cases not covered? Custom values suggesting missing scale steps?). Process: 1) Inventory — extract all visual values from code and design files. 2) Categorise — group by type (colour, spacing, typography, etc.). 3) Map — match values to existing tokens. 4) Flag — identify hard-coded values, mismatches, gaps. 5) Prioritise — rank by frequency and impact. 6) Recommend — new tokens, migrations, cleanup. Report format: executive summary with adoption percentage, detailed findings by category, hard-coded inventory with replacements, recommended new tokens, migration plan. Audit both design files and code. Automate detection with lint rules. Focus on high-impact categories first (colour, spacing). Track adoption over time.

title: Design Presentations — Communicating and Persuading
content: Effective presentations are structured for the audience. Types: Stakeholder update (inform/align — context, progress, decisions, next steps, asks), Design review (get feedback — objectives, walkthrough, rationale, open questions), Final showcase (gain approval — problem, process, solution, evidence, impact), Portfolio/case study (demonstrate capability — challenge, approach, decisions, outcome). Universal structure: 1) Hook — why should they care? 2) Context — what do they need to know? 3) Journey — how did you get here? 4) Solution — what are you proposing? 5) Evidence — why is this right? 6) Ask — what do you need from them? Slide principles: one idea per slide, show don't tell, progressive disclosure, design for the back of the room, include speaker notes. Audience adaptation: Executives (lead with impact, be concise, business value), Engineers (technical details, specs, edge cases), Designers (process, rationale, system alignment), Mixed (layer progressively, big picture first). Rehearse with a colleague. Prepare backup slides for questions. End with a clear ask.

title: UX Writing — Interface Copy That Guides Users
content: UX writing categories: Microcopy (button labels — action-oriented and specific, form labels — clear and concise, tooltips — brief explanations, placeholder text — example format not instructions). Error messages (what happened — clear not technical, why — if helpful, what to do — specific action, human tone — not blaming). Empty states (explain what will appear, guide to action, encouraging tone, clear CTA). Confirmation messages (confirm what happened, next steps if relevant, undo for reversible actions, brief and positive). Onboarding copy (welcome without overwhelming, one concept at a time, action-oriented, allow skipping). CTAs (start with a verb, be specific about outcome, match user intent not business intent). Voice is consistent (brand personality, vocabulary, perspective); Tone varies by context (celebration vs error vs instruction). Writing principles: clear over clever, concise over comprehensive, helpful over promotional, consistent over creative, inclusive over casual. Write copy before designing the UI — content-first. Test copy with users. Create a terminology dictionary. Consider translation and localisation from the start.

title: Data Visualisation — Chart Selection and Accessible Design
content: Chart selection by purpose: Comparison (bar charts, grouped bars, bullet charts), Trend over time (line charts, area charts, sparklines), Part of whole (pie/donut for few categories, stacked bar for many, treemap for hierarchical), Distribution (histogram, box plot, scatter plot), Relationship (scatter plot, bubble chart, heat map). Design principles: maximise data-ink ratio (Edward Tufte), clear axis labels and legends, consistent colour encoding, start y-axis at zero for bar charts, annotate to highlight insights. Colour: sequential (light-to-dark for ordered data), diverging (two-hue for above/below midpoint), categorical (distinct hues for unrelated categories), always use colourblind-safe palettes. Accessibility: don't rely on colour alone — use patterns, labels, or shapes; provide text alternatives; keyboard-navigable interactive charts; sufficient contrast. Responsive: simplify at small sizes, consider table alternatives for mobile, touch-friendly tooltips. Choose the simplest chart that communicates the insight. Label directly on chart when possible. Test with real data.
source: Edward Tufte — The Visual Display of Quantitative Information

title: Illustration Style Systems — Visual Language and Rules
content: An illustration style guide ensures consistent visual storytelling. Style definition axes: geometric vs organic, flat vs dimensional (2D/2.5D/3D), detailed vs minimal, abstract vs representational, line style (stroke weight, corners, endpoints). Colour: use a subset of the product colour palette, define primary/secondary/accent illustration colours, rules for gradients and shadows, dark mode variants. Character design (if applicable): proportions, face detail level, diversity and representation guidelines, poses and expressions library. Illustration types: spot illustrations (small, inline, supporting UI), hero illustrations (large, featured, storytelling), empty states (guide users), onboarding (explain features), error states (soften messages). Application rules: when to use vs when not to, size constraints per context, alignment with grid system, animation guidelines. Create reusable element libraries. Document the creation process for contributors. Test at intended display sizes. Don't convey critical information only through illustrations — always pair with text for accessibility.

title: Visual Hierarchy — Guiding Attention Through Design
content: Visual hierarchy ensures users see the most important content first. Tools: Size (larger draws attention — use 1.5× minimum for clear distinction), Weight (bold text, thicker strokes, filled icons carry more visual weight), Colour and contrast (high contrast attracts — use strategically for CTAs, status, emphasis), Spacing (more whitespace around an element increases perceived importance), Position (top-left in LTR gets seen first, above fold matters, F-pattern and Z-pattern scanning), Density (isolated elements stand out, grouped elements scan as a unit). Four hierarchy levels: Primary (page title, primary CTA — seen first), Secondary (section headings, key content — scanned next), Tertiary (supporting text, metadata — read on demand), Quaternary (fine print, timestamps — available but not prominent). Patterns: hero sections (large type + image + single CTA), cards (image > title > description > action), forms (label > input > helper > error), navigation (current > available > disabled). The squint test: blur your eyes — hierarchy should still be clear. One primary action per view. Don't compete for attention — choose what matters most.
